coredata.dc:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions
coredata.dc:creator: Bai, Hanyu
Tu, Zaiqian
Liu, Yitong
Tai, Qunxi
Guo, Zhongkai
Liu, Siyu
coredata.dc:description: The pollution of heavy metal increases greatly accompanying by the development of industries. So, it is very important to build up a quick and reliable technique for detection of heavy metal ions. In this work, we developed a simple and convenient method for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions. We utilized glutathione as raw material to prepare cyan-emitting carbon dots (GSH@CDs). The GSH@CDs were further used as the template to prepare carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH@CDs-Cu NCs) that displayed two well-separated emission peaks respectively at 450 nm and 750 nm. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs can be applied for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions based on the fluorescence quenching or enhancement of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs at 750 nm. A linear range of 2–40 μmol L-1 with a detection limit of 0.9 μmol L-1 for Cr2O7 2- ions, and a linear range of 0–20 μmol L-1 with a detection limit of 0.6 μmol L-1 for Cd2+ ions were achieved based on this method. The fluorescent test strips for Cr2O7 2- ions were successfully prepared based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Moreover, the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were successfully applied to determine Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions in real samples with promising results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dual emission
Carbon dots
Copper nanoclusters
Cr2O7 2-ions
Cd2+ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
The preparation of fluorescent test strips and detecting applications
Synthesis and characterization of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
The stability of the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr2O7 2+ ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cd2+ ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates ( 201910145108 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( N182410001 ) . Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions upon the various fluorescence excitations wavelengths. (B) The fluorescence excitation spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs with emission at 450 nm (a), emission at 750 nm (b) and the UV–vis absorption spectra (c) of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs.
(A) The TEM images and particle size distributions of (A) GSH-Cu NCs and (B) GSH@CDs-Cu NCs.

(A) The fluorescence emission intensity at 450 nm or 750 nm of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions in different pH environments (pH 11.8, 11.0, 10.2, 9.4, 8.6, 7.8, 7.0, 6.2, 5.4, 4.6, 3.8, 3.0, 2.2). (B) The fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions under various pH environments. Inset: Linear relationship between F450/F750 versus pH value in the range of 6.2-7.0. Photo: The images of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions respectively in pH 6.2 and 7.0 environment visualized by UV light.

(A)The fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions respectively with increasing Cr2O7 2- ions concentrations. (a: 0, b: 2, c: 5, d: 10, e: 20, f: 40, g: 60, h: 90, i: 120 μmol L-1). Inset: Linear relationship between F450/F750 versus Cr2O7 2- ions concentrations in the range of 2−40 μmol L-1. The images of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions respectively with a: 0, b: 20, c: 40, d: 60, e: 90 μmol L-1 Cr2O7 2- ions under (B) visible light (C) ultraviolet light.

(A) The fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions respectively with increasing Cd2+ ions concentrations. (a: 0, b: 2, c: 5, d: 10, e: 20, f: 40, g: 60, h: 80 μmol L-1). Inset: Linear relationship between F750/F450 versus Cd2+ ions concentrations in the range of 0−20 μmol L-1. The images of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions respectively with a: 0, b: 10, c: 20, d: 40, e: 80 μmol L-1 Cd2+ ions concentrations under (B) visible light (C) ultraviolet light.
The schematic illustration of the preparation of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs and the application in the detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions.

head.ce:title: Dual-emission carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life and Health Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Life and Health Sciences, Northeastern University, Shenyang, 110000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The pollution of heavy metal increases greatly accompanying by the development of industries. So, it is very important to build up a quick and reliable technique for detection of heavy metal ions. In this work, we developed a simple and convenient method for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions. We utilized glutathione as raw material to prepare cyan-emitting carbon dots (GSH@CDs). The GSH@CDs were further used as the template to prepare carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH@CDs-Cu NCs) that displayed two well-separated emission peaks respectively at 450 nm and 750 nm. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs can be applied for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions based on the fluorescence quenching or enhancement of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs at 750 nm. A linear range of 2–40 μmol L-1 with a detection limit of 0.9 μmol L-1 for Cr2O7 2- ions, and a linear range of 0–20 μmol L-1 with a detection limit of 0.6 μmol L-1 for Cd2+ ions were achieved based on this method. The fluorescent test strips for Cr2O7 2- ions were successfully prepared based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Moreover, the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were successfully applied to determine Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions in real samples with promising results.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industrialization, heavy metals contamination has become an increasing environmental problem in the worldwide. These heavy metal ions usually possess high toxicities, and threaten human body through the enriched food chains (Bansod et al., 2017). Dichromate (Cr2O7 2-), as a heavy metal pollutant, has been listed as a highly dangerous toxic substance by United States Environment Protection Agency (Lu et al., 2017). If the content of Cr (VI) in water is in excess of 0.1 mg L-1, human organs such as stomachs, lung, liver and kidneys would be seriously harmed (He et al., 2018a; Rakhunde et al., 2012). Cadmium is another common heavy metal pollutant, and cadmium exposure can cause series of diseases such as renal dysfunction and cancers (Chen et al., 2017; Guo et al., 2014). The recommended concentration for Cd2+ ions in drinking water is not exceeding 3 ng·mL-1 by World Health Organization (Huang et al., 2017). Waste Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions cannot be degraded naturally, and they are easy to accumulate in organisms (Yang et al., 2018a; Pan et al., 2018). Therefore, it is of importance to develop a rapid and reliable technique for detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions for human health and the environmental protection.

Many techniques including atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, and etc. have been used to detect the contents of heavy metals in complex matrices (Bansod et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018a). Over the past two decades, a great variety of fluorescent sensors have been developed for detecting various kinds of metal ions due to its high sensitivity and low cost (Rani and John, 2018; Zhou et al., 2017; Li et al., 2018b; Gao et al., 2018; Parmar et al., 2017a, b). In recent years, ratio fluorescent assays based on the ratio changes of fluorescence signal at two different wavelengths with or without analytes have attracted a great of attentions (Chen et al., 2018a). Ratiometric fluorescent measurements cannot only improve the detection sensitivity but also eliminate some interference factors such as probe concentration, the fluctuation of light source and photobleaching (Santhakumar et al., 2018; Ast et al., 2017). Another potential advantage of ratiometric fluorescent measurement is that the changes of fluorescence at different wavelengths may give rise to visual fluorescent color changes, which offers the potential of naked-eye detection for analytes (Chen et al., 2018b; Xu et al., 2018; Liang et al., 2017). However, most ratiometric fluorescent probes required complicated producing process and long preparation period, which limited their mass production and further application. Because of the relatively low price and abundant reserves of copper, the cost for copper nanoclusters (Cu NCs) preparation is usually far below the cost of silver and gold nanoclusters preparation (Wang et al., 2018; Ai et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017a; Patel et al., 2018). Carbon dots, as a rising star of fluorescent nanomaterials, can be prepared conveniently based a variety of starting materials, which exhibited adjustable emission wavelengths and excellent photostability and chemical stability (Ding et al., 2017; Han et al., 2017; Ma et al., 2016a). Therefore, the hybrid materials composed of carbon dots and Cu NCs are expected to become an ideal fluorescence ratiometric probe with advantages of both materials for analytes detection.

In this work, we firstly utilized glutathione (GSH) as original materials to prepare cyan-emitting carbon dots (GSH@CDs). The GSH@CDs were further used as template to prepare carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters (GSH@CDs-Cu NCs), which displayed dual emission wavelengths respectively at 450 nm and 750 nm. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were used as ratiometric fluorescence probe for detection of Cr2O7 2- ions and Cd2+ ions, respectively.

Glutathione, Tri-hydroxymethyl aminomethane (Tris), hydrochloric acid (HCl) and various kinds of metal salts were all obtained from Bejing Dingguo Biotechnology Company. All reagents used were of analytical grade.

The GSH@CDs were prepared by the following steps：360 mg glutathione was dissolved in 12 mL of deionized water, and then transferred into a 25 mL teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, which was then placed in the oven at 140 ℃ for 4 h. At the end of the heating period, the autoclave was naturally cooled to room temperature. After the centrifugation with the speed of 12, 000 rpm, the yellow supernatant was collected. The purified GSH@CDs powder was collected by dialysis and lyophilization.

The GSH-Cu NCs were prepared according to previous report (Luo et al., 2015). GSH solution (5.0 mL, 25 mg mL-1) was added into CuCl2 solution (5.0 mL, 10 mmol·L-1). Then, NaOH solution (2 mol·L-1) was added dropwise to adjust solutions in the pH range 4-5. Subsequently, the solution was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 2 h to obtain the GSH-Cu NCs solutions.

GSH@CDs solution (5 mL, 25 mg mL-1) was added into 5 mL of CuCl2 solution (10 mmol·L-1). Then, NaOH solution (2 mol·L-1) was added dropwise until the white turbid liquid turned to transparent, and the solution pH value was adjusted to 4-5. Subsequently, the solution was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 2 h. The upper solution was collected by centrifugation with the speed of 12, 000 rpm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: For detection of Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ ions concentrations in solutions, 60 μL of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solution, 40 μL of Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ ions solutions with various concentrations were added into 100 μL of tris−HCl buffer solutions (pH 6.2, 20 mmol·L-1) with incubation time of 15 min, and then the fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of these mixtures were measured and recorded.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation of fluorescent test strips and detecting applications
ce:section.ce:para: In order to prepare GSH@CDs-Cu NCs-based test strips, qualitative filter papers were cut into pieces of squares with a side of length 10 mm. The prepared test strips were immersed into GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solution for 1 h, and wait to naturally dry. The test strips were pasted in a black plastic sheet, and the prepared test strips were wetted by 20 μL of different solutions with various metal ions concentrations. The changes of fluorescent color of the test strips were directly observed and recorded under an ultraviolet lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1 , the GSH@CDs was prepared with GSH as the original material by one-pot hydrothermal synthesis. Similar to some previous reports, the prepared GSH@CDs displayed an obvious excitation-dependent feature (Lu et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2017; Shangguan et al., 2017) (Fig.S1). Therefore, the fluorescence mechanisms of GSH@CDs can be attributed to the composing of carbogenic core state emission (Xu et al., 2017). The prepared GSH@CDs were further used as the reducing reagent and template for preparing Cu NCs in aqueous solution. Compared to previous reports about dual-emission fluorescent nanohybrids based on fluorescent carbon dots and copper nanoclusters, we proposed a simple strategy to obtain GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, which can avoid complicated chemical coupling and modifications (Chen et al., 2018a; Zou et al., 2016; He et al., 2018b).

Fig. 1 A showed that the position of fluorescence emission peak at 750 nm of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs upon different excitation wavelengths could remain unchanged, which was due to the characteristic emission of copper nanoclusters (Lin et al., 2017). Fig. 1B respectively offered the fluorescence excitation and UV–vis absorption spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs displayed two excitation band respectively around 350 nm (emission at 450 nm) and 380 nm (emission at 750 nm), and there are some overlap between the two excitation bands. Therefore, GSH@CDs-Cu NCs displayed two emission peaks respectively at 450 nm and 750 nm with the single excitation wavelength of 360 nm. We compared the fluorescent emission spectra of GSH-Cu NCs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs (Fig.S2). The GSH-Cu NCs exhibited a single emission peak at 650 nm, and the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs displayed two well-separated emission peak at 450 nm and 750 nm, which had a great emission separation of 300 nm.

The morphology and structure of GSH-Cu NCs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were further characterized in Fig. 2 . The observed GSH-Cu NCs exhibited a narrow diameter distribution of 1.56–3.59 nm with an average particle size of 2.29 nm. Compared to GSH-Cu NCs, GSH@CDs-Cu NCs had a wider diameter distribution in the range from 1.76 to 5.25 nm, and a larger average size of 3.01 nm. Fig.S3 respectively provided the zeta-potential measurements of GSH@CDs, GSH-Cu NCs, GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. The surface electric potential of GSH@CDs was 10.5 mV, and the surface potential of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs changed from 10.5 mV to -1.5 mV. Fig.S4 provided the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of GSH, GSH-Cu NCs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Compared with the XRD pattern of GSH powder, the characteristic diffraction peaks respectively at 43° and 50° attributed to Cu NCs were observed in the XRD patterns of GSH-Cu NCs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. The multiple peaks in the range from 15 to 40° of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs patterns can be attributed to the coordination between Cu NCs core and GSH@CDs shell.

The FT-IR spectrum of GSH, GSH@CDs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were respectively provided in Fig.S5. The FT-IR spectrum of GSH (Fig.S5 curve a) displays stretching vibrations for OH/N-H (3300 cm-1), 2550 cm-1 (-SH), −CH2- (1495 cm-1), CO (1670 cm-1) and C–N (1390 cm-1). Besides the peaks mentioned above, the emerging peaks around 1590 cm-1, 1090 cm-1, 705 cm-1 that can be identified as the stretching vibrations for CC, CS, −CH = CH- were respectively found in the spectrum of GSH@CDs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, which illustrated the severe carbonization of GSH@CDs (Fig.S5 curve b and curve c). Besides, disappearance of -SH stretching vibration characteristic peak proved the formation of the covalent bonds between GSH@CDs and Cu NCs core (Ma et al., 2016b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The stability of the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Scheme 1, the prepared GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were stabilized by the matrix of GSH@CDs (Ding et al., 2018; Lv et al., 2019; Gao et al., 2019; Jiang et al., 2017). We further investigated the photochemical stability and colloidal stability of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs in various environments. The photobleaching curves of the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions exposure to continuous UV irradiation were offered in Fig.S6. The fluorescence emission intensity at 750 nm (F750) and 450 nm (F450) both remained basically stable exposed to continuous ultraviolet irradiation for 90 min. Therefore, the dual emission intensity of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs had the potential for continuous tracking studies. Herein, we further investigated the effects of ionic strength, various molecules and pH environments on the dual emission intensity F450 and F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Fig. S7 described F450 and F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs incubated with increasing NaCl or KCl concentration from 0 to 1000 mmol·L-1 (mM) for 30 min. The dual emission intensity F450 and F750 were almost the same even incubated with 1 mol·L-1 (M) NaCl or KCl, and high ionic strength media only had negligible effect on the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Fig.S8 further indicated the dual emission intensity of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs had no obvious response to various molecules (0.2 mmol·L-1) and proteins (0.2 mg mL-1).

Based on some previous reports on copper nanoclusters, pH environment usually has great influence on copper nanoclusters (Lin et al., 2017; Boonmee et al., 2018). Therefore, we investigated the effects of solution pH on the dual emission intensity of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. As shown in Fig. 3 A, F450 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs gradually enhanced as the pH value increased from 2.2–11.8. In contrast, F750 of was nearly invariable in the pH range from 2.2–6.2, and displayed a dramatic decrease with pH increase from 6.2–7.0. Fig. 3B indicates that there exists a good linear relationship between the ratios F450/F750 and pH value in the range from 6.2–7.0 as the following equations:
F450/F750 = 4.7298[pH]-28.3633 (1)

Therefore, the prepared GSH@CDs-Cu NCs can be also used for ratiometric detection of pH in the range from 6.2–7.0. The photo in Fig. 3B showed that the fluorescent colors of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions under ultraviolet lamp changed from yellow-green to green with the pH value increasing from 6.2–7.0. The DLS measurements of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs respectively in pH 6.2, 7.0 and 7.8 environments were offered in Fig.S9. We can see that the hydrodynamic size of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions showed a significant increase with the increase of pH from in the range from 6.2–7.8. The UV–vis absorption spectra and color changes under daylight of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs in different pH environments were shown in Fig.S10. A new surface plasmon band around 580 nm significantly emerged as the pH increased from 6.2–7.8, and the color of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solution under daylight changed from yellowish to light green, which indicated the formation of larger copper nanoparticles according to previous reports (Hatamie et al., 2014). The fluorescence intensity F450 can remain stable with the pH value increased from 7.0–11.8, whereas the F750 vanished completely when the pH value was more than 7.0. The above results indicated that the GSH@CDs shell can be stable in the pH value in the wide range from 2.2–11.8, and the Cu NCs core would further grow into copper nanoparticles stabilized by GSH@CDs shell with pH greater than 7.0.

We further investigated the fluorescence changes of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions under various temperatures environment. Fig.S11 demonstrated that along with the rise of temperature from 25 ℃ to 55 ℃, F450 decreased slightly, and F750 displayed a dramatic quenching in the range from 40 to 55 ℃. The stable fluorescence of F450 indicated the stability of GSH@CDs under various temperatures, and the fluorescence quenching of F750 demonstrated the sensitivity of Cu NCs core to higher temperature that was originated from its aggregation-induced emission behavior and temperature-dependent solubility of Cu NCs in water (Ding et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017b). Furthermore, we explored the solvent stability of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Fig.S12 indicated that the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs would aggregate and accumulate on the bottom in methanol, ethanol, acetone or acetonitrile solution, and the fluorescence intensity F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs enhanced significantly. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs can be destroyed by isopropanol solution, and the fluorescence intensity F750 attributed to Cu NCs was quenched sharply. The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs can remain stable in water and N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cr2O7 2+ ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para:
We further examined the influence of various common metal ions on the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs under the same conditions. The fluorescence emission intensity F450 or F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs remains almost unchanged in the presence of most of tested metal ions (Fig.S13). It is noticeable that F750 respectively displayed an obvious fluorescence quenching in the presence of 60 μmol L-1 Cr2O7 2- and a fluorescence enhancement in the presence of 60 μmol L-1 Cd2+ ions (Fig.S13). Fig. 4 A offered the fluorescence emission spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs with various Cr2O7 2- concentrations. With Cr2O7 2- concentration enhancement, F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs gradually reduced, and F450had no significant changes. Furthermore, Fig. 4AInset demonstrated that the relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio F450/F750 and Cr2O7 2- concentrations in the range from 2–40 μmol L-1 obeyed the following equations:
F450/F750 = 1.0775 + 0.0160[Cr2O7 2-], (μmol·L-1) (2)

The correlation coefficient is 0.995, and the detection limit for Cr2O7 2- assay is 0.9 μmol L-1 calculated following the 3σ IUPAC criteria. Furthermore, Fig. 4B provided the photos of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions incubated with various Cr2O7 2- concentrations under daylight. As Cr2O7 2- concentrations increased, the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions changed from yellowish transparent to turbid under daylight, which showed that the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were gradually destroyed by Cr2O7 2- ions. Under ultraviolet lamp, the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions changed from yellow-green fluorescence to green fluorescence with the increasing Cr2O7 2- concentrations accordingly (Fig. 4C). Fig.S14 showed that the absorption band of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs enhanced remarkably as the Cr2O7 2- concentrations increased from 0 to 120 μmol L-1. The dynamic laser scattering measurements indicated that the hydrated size of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs showed a significant reduce with increasing Cr2O7 2- concentrations (Fig.S15). The fluorescence emission peak at 750 nm of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs that can be attributed to the characteristic emission of copper nanoclusters came from the luminescence of aggregation-induced emission. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching at 750 nm induced by Cr2O7 2- ions can be attributed to the disruption of the inter-Cu(I) interactions (Goswami et al., 2016). As shown in Fig.S16 and Fig.S17, we further investigated the effects of chromate anion (CrO4 2- ions) on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Similar to Cr2O7 2- ions, CrO4 2- ions also can quench the fluorescence intensity F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, and the relationship between the ratio F450/F750 and CrO4 2- ions concentrations in the range from 12.5–200 μmol L-1 obeyed the following equations:
F450/F750 = 1.0672 + 0.0076[CrO4 2-], (μmol·L-1) (3)

The correlation coefficient of linear equations is 0.994, and the detection limit for CrO4 2- assay is 2.3 μmol L-1 calculated following the 3σ IUPAC criteria. Along with the increase of CrO4 2- concentrations from 0 to 250 μmol L-1, the absorption band of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs also enhanced significantly (Fig.S17). Therefore, GSH@CDs-Cu NCs displayed a more sensitive response to Cr2O7 2- ions than CrO4 2- ions.

In order to test the anti-interference capability of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs for Cr2O7 2- ions detection, we respectively investigated the fluorescent response of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs to 60 μmol L-1 Cr2O7 2- in the presence of various metal ions (Fig.S18). The results showed that the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs had strong anti-interference capability for Cr2O7 2- detection. Therefore, the prepared GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were suitable for ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- ions.

We further investigated the influence of ascorbic acid on the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs in the presence of Cr2O7 2- ions. The quenched fluorescence by Cr2O7 2- ions can be gradually recovered as ascorbic acid concentrations increased from 0 to 300 μmol L-1 (Fig.S19). Ascorbic acid, as a natural antioxidant, can reduce Cr2O7 2- ions to generate Cr3+ ions. Fig.S8 and Fig.S13 indicated that Cr2O7 2- ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, but Cr3+ ions and ascorbic acid cannot affect the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. Therefore, ascorbic acid can effectively eliminate the interference from Cr2O7 2- ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The ratiometric fluorescent detection of Cd2+ ions based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs
ce:section.ce:para:
We further studied the effects of Cd2+ ions concentrations on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. F750 of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs gradually enhanced, and F450can remain unchanged along with Cd2+ ions concentration increasing (Fig. 5 A). As shown in Fig. 5AInset, the relationship between the ratio F750/F450 and Cd2+ concentrations in the range from 0 to 20 μmol L-1 can be described well as the following equations:
F750/F450 = 0.9034 + 0.0264 [Cd2+], (μmol·L-1) (4)

The correlation coefficients of regression equations is 0.992, and the detection limit of Cd2+ is 0.6 μmol L-1 calculated following the 3σ IUPAC criteria. Fig. 5B and Fig. 5C indicated that as Cd2+ concentration increased, the color of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions almost kept the same under daylight, but the fluorescence color of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions gradually changed from yellow-green to pink under ultraviolet light. Fig.S20 displayed the effects of various Cd2+ ions concentrations on the absorption spectra of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. It can be found that the absorption of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs showed only slight reduction in the range from 350 to 850 nm with the increase of Cd2+ concentrations, and did not appear new absorption peaks. The zeta-potential measurements showed that the charges on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were neutralized by addition of Cd2+ ions, which indicated that Cd2+ ions were absorbed on the surface of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs to neutralize the surface charge (Fig.S21). The dynamic laser scattering measurements also demonstrated that there existed an obvious increase in the hydrated size of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs with the rise of Cd2+ ions concentrations (Fig.S22). The above results indicated that GSH@CDs-Cu NCs tended to aggregate through coordinating with Cd2+ ions. The formed aggregate induced by Cd2+ ions may reduce the energy loss to enhance the emission efficiency through the nonradiative rotational relaxation channel (Lin et al., 2017; Jalili and Khataee, 2019).

In order to test the anti-interference capability of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs for Cd2+ ions detection, we respectively investigated the fluorescent response of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs to Cd2+ ions in the presence of various metal ions. Fig.S23 demonstrated that the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs had strong anti-interference capability for Cd2+ detection. As provided in Table S1, we summarized various fluorescent materials for detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions. We can see that the current method based on GSH@CDs-Cu NCs offered similar or more excellent detection range and detection limit, and had a simple preparation process.

The recyclability experiments were performed to test the recyclable potential of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs for detection of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions. In order to test recyclable potential for detection of Cr2O7 2- ions, excess acetonitrile were added into the mixed solution composed of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs and 60 μmol L-1 Cr2O7 2- ions. The precipitates were collected after centrifugation with the speed of 12, 000 rpm, and then dissolve in deionized water again, which were used for the next cycle for Cr2O7 2- ions detection. It can be seen that Cr2O7 2- ions can still quench the fluorescence at 750 nm of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs at Run 2 and Run 3, but the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs weakened sharply after repeated cycles (Fig.S24A). The recyclable potential for detection of Cd2+ ions were shown in Fig.S24B. We can find that Cd2+ ions can enhance the fluorescence at 750 nm of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs at Run 1, Run 2 and Run 3, and the fluorescence of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs remained well after repeated cycles. The XRD patterns of pristine GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, GSH@CDs-Cu NCs respectively treated with Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ ions were all provided in Fig.S25. Compared with the XRD pattern of pristine GSH@CDs-Cu NCs, the multiple peaks in the range from 15° to 40° weakened after being treated with Cr2O7 2- ions, which indicated the coordination between Cu NCs core and GSH@CDs shell were destroyed by Cr2O7 2- ions. In contrast, the XRD patterns of pristine GSH@CDs-Cu NCs and GSH@CDs-Cu NCs treated with Cd2+ ions were almost the same, indicating the structure of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs could remain stable after being treated with Cd2+ ions.

In order to expand the applications of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs probe, fluorescent test strips were prepared for the convenient detection of Cr2O7 2- ion or Cd2+ ions. Fig.S26 and Fig.S27 respectively displayed the fluorescent colors changes of test strips wetted by 20 μL of Cr2O7 2- ions or Cd2+ ions solutions with various concentrations. We can find that the test strips showed a fluorescent colors changes from pink to purple under UV light, when they were respectively wetted by increasing Cr2O7 2- concentrations (Fig.S26). As shown in Fig.S27, only an irregular color changes under UV light were observed when the test strips were wetted by various concentrations of Cd2+ ions, which was not consistent with the color changes of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs solutions with various Cd2+ ions concentrations under UV light(Fig. 5C) (Yang et al., 2018b; Jiang et al., 2018; Huang et al., 2019). As described above, the Cd2+-induced fluorescent enhancement at 750 nm can be attributed to the aggregations of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs. When the GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were fixed on the filter papers, the migration of GSH@CDs-Cu NCs on the test strips were restrained. Therefore, the prepared test strips were only suitable for rapid detection of Cr2O7 2- ions.

The GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were further utilized for the determination of Cr2O7 2- and Cd2+ ions in tap water, spring water samples and human urine samples with the standard addition method. The water samples passed through a 0.45 μm filter membrane before use. Different concentrations of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions were added to the water samples, and the detection results were offered in Table S2 and Table S3. The recoveries of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ based on the developed methods are respectively in the range from 102 % to 109 % and from 104 % to 110 % with the relative standard deviations (RSD) less than 4.5 %. The healthy man’s urine samples were subjected to a 10-fold dilution and spiked with different concentrations of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions. The recoveries of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ based on the developed methods in urine samples are in the range from 97 % to 108 % with the RSD less than 3.5 % (Table S4 and Table S5). The above results demonstrated the feasibility of this method for the detection of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions in different real samples.

We developed the novel carbon dots-stabilized copper nanoclusters with dual emission peaks at 450 and 750 nm. The prepared GSH@CDs-Cu NCs were respectively used for the ratiometric and visual detection of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions, and were successfully used for the determination of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions concentrations in real samples. Therefore, the prepared GSH@CDs-Cu NCs provided a simple and quick method for detection of Cr2O7 2- or Cd2+ ions, which showed great potential in environmental monitoring and biochemical analysis.
The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.
We declare that we do not have any commercial or associative interest that represents a conflict of interest in connection with the work submitted.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Training Program of Innovation and Entrepreneurship for Undergraduates (201910145108) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities(N182410001) .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2019.121654.
